Navigating modern love in your AI era.I Asked AI What Makes a Relationship Last. The Answer Wasn’t What I Expected.
When people hear the words Artificial Intelligence, they usually think about writing code, generating images, or answering questions.
One evening, I asked something completely different.
I asked about relationships.
Not because I expected AI to tell me who I should love.
I was simply curious whether it could help me organize something that has puzzled human beings for thousands of years.
What makes a relationship worth building?
The conversation surprised me.
Not because AI gave me magical answers.
Because it helped me ask better questions.
Instead of talking about finding “the perfect partner,” our conversation slowly evolved into a simple framework.
BE
Who is this person becoming?
Not their job.
Not their appearance.
Not their social media profile.
Who are they becoming as a human being?
DO
This was the part that stayed with me.
We often choose partners because of what they have.
Good looks.
A successful career.
Money.
Cooking skills.
Shared hobbies.
Those things can certainly matter.
But they aren’t usually what determines whether we feel happy years later.
The deeper question is:
How does this person make my life feel?
Do I feel safe?
Do I feel understood?
Do I feel calmer after spending time with them?
Do I feel accepted?
Do I feel more alive?
Do I become a better version of myself?
Perhaps those feelings matter just as much as any impressive résumé.
HAVE
Only after that comes the future.
Can we build something together?
Can we solve problems together?
Can we support one another when life becomes difficult?
Can we create a life that both of us are proud to share?
The Part That Changed My Thinking
The final idea was the simplest.
Every relationship is an exchange.
Not an exchange of money.
An exchange of emotional value.
Every day we give something.
Time.
Trust.
Patience.
Compromise.
Vulnerability.
Hopefully, we also receive something.
Belonging.
Safety.
Joy.
Peace.
Encouragement.
If, over time, the emotional value we receive is greater than the emotional cost of maintaining the relationship, most people naturally want to stay.
If that balance disappears for a long time, relationships often become difficult.
I had never looked at relationships quite that way before.
What AI Didn’t Do
AI didn’t tell me whom to love.
It didn’t predict my future.
It didn’t replace my judgment.
It simply helped organize ideas into a structure that made me think more clearly.
And perhaps that’s one of the best uses of artificial intelligence.
Not replacing human wisdom.
Helping us discover it.
That conversation also made me smile.
We really are living in an AI era.
It even inspired one of my T-shirt designs: “I’m in My AI Era.”
To me, that phrase isn’t about computers taking over the world.
It means staying curious enough to ask better questions.
Because technology may continue changing at incredible speed.
But understanding ourselves — and each other — will probably always remain the most fascinating conversation of all.
